costs: Early diagnostic and remedial support system build-out — high, recurring; no numeric fiscal range has been published in Hungarian sources, though Portugal's grade-retention reduction (analogous compensatory investment) saved EUR 112 million over two years across 663 schools, offering one partial cross-national cost/benefit reference point of a different but related intervention.

Teacher retraining and reallocation for a unified comprehensive lower-secondary phase, high cost given the documented aging workforce and acute regional shortages, magnitude unquantified in published Hungarian costing studies.
Facility conversion and capital costs for consolidating parallel 4/6/8-year track infrastructure into a unified structure, unquantified.
Anti-sorting enforcement infrastructure for school-choice and catchment regulation, a wholly new cost category with no prior Hungarian analogue or published estimate.
content.equity_impact: The scenario's largest identified risk is that sorting relocates into school choice, non-state maintainer expansion, catchment-area effects, and private tutoring markets, potentially neutralizing any equity gain from raising the selection age alone; Poland's own experience shows between-school equity improved sharply while individual-level SES gaps did not close, and Finland's post-2000 erosion via school choice demonstrates this is not a hypothetical risk but an observed pattern in both reference cases — the scenario's anti-sorting rules for choice and catchment are the load-bearing, currently unproven component without which the headline equity claim does not hold.
expected_benefits: Potential achievement gains in the range of Poland's observed 0.16-0.28 SD across subjects, with the largest gains for at-risk/vocational-track-bound students, if Hungary's context proves sufficiently comparable.

Reduced intergenerational income elasticity, by analogy with Finland's observed 23% reduction (0.30 to 0.23) following its delay of tracking from 11 to 16, though Finland's effect was bundled with welfare-state expansion and teacher-education reform not isolated from the tracking-age effect itself.

Between-school equity improvement of the magnitude Poland achieved (moving from one of the OECD's largest between-school disparities to one of the smallest) is plausible given Hungary's currently even higher baseline segregation, offering a larger absolute gap to close.
content.goal: Raise the statutory age of first academic tracking to 14+ by phasing out 6- and 8-year gimnázium entry points into a unified comprehensive lower-secondary phase, paired with explicit anti-sorting rules for school choice and catchment.

implementation_steps.action: Draft and negotiate legislation raising the statutory secondary-education age boundary (amending Nkt. §4), with cross-party consultation attempted given the documented reversal risk of single-party structural reforms (Poland's 18-year reversal precedent).

Design felmenő-rendszer (grade-by-grade) phase-out schedule protecting already-admitted cohorts, plus anti-sorting rules for school choice and catchment to be enacted simultaneously, not sequentially.

Staged, capacity-realistic teacher-retraining programme sequenced after — or explicitly coordinated with — the 2024-2031 salary-reform timeline, acknowledging that current CPD bandwidth is already committed to NAT and AI-integration reforms and that rural/STEM shortages are 2-3 times more severe, meaning full national rollout within this window remains ambitious and may require regional staggering beyond the stated end date.
Enforce and monitor anti-sorting rules for school choice and catchment, publishing annual segregation-index reports to detect displacement of sorting into unregulated channels.

mechanisms: Cross-national difference-in-differences evidence associates earlier between-school tracking with greater inequality of outcomes without raising mean achievement, providing the core rationale for delaying selection.

Poland's 1999 reform (delaying selection from age 12 to 15) produced PISA gains of 0.25 SD math, 0.28 SD reading, 0.16 SD science, with at-risk students gaining approximately 1 SD, directly demonstrating the achievable effect-size range for structural delay.

Sorting does not simply migrate to school choice, non-state maintainers, catchment effects, or private tutoring markets to the point of neutralizing the equity gain only if explicit anti-sorting rules for choice and catchment are implemented and enforced simultaneously with the structural delay; absent such rules, Kertesi & Kezdi (2013) and Berenyi-Berkovits-Erőss school-choice research directly document that free school choice plus early selective tracks already produce strong between-school sorting at the primary stage, and Finland's post-2000 experience shows equity gains eroding via school choice and residential sorting even after successful de-tracking.
risks: The scenario identifies a substantial reversal risk citing the Polish precedent (reform reversed after 18 years despite documented gains), and the only entrenchment device contemplated (a cross-party political pact) is not specified with statutory safeguards, independent monitoring triggers, or supermajority requirements, leaving the reform structurally exposed in the same way Poland's was.

Fidesz's immediate framing of any 'unified model' as totalitarian ('no example since communism since') establishes this scenario as the highest-visibility target for culture-war opposition among all five, given it most closely matches the minister's initially floated (then retracted) unified four-year model idea.

Church and foundation maintainer opposition is likely to be intense given the scenario would phase out the entry points (grades 5 and 7) that anchor their fastest-growing enrollment segment, and Tisza's coalition commitment to preserve prior church-maintainer operating conditions creates an internal contradiction with this scenario's core mechanism.
content.title: Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)
